testcase: Repo Access Question
query:
title: Repo Access Question

question: Is there a specific group to join or process I need to follow in order to get access to https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr repo?
ground_truth:
Yes, to get access to the https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr repository, you need to join the Azure organization on GitHub. Specifically, you should:
Join the Azure organization: Go to https://repos.opensource.microsoft.com/orgs/Azure and click 'Join Organization'.
Ensure your GitHub membership in the Azure organization is set to PUBLIC visibility, as private memberships cannot be verified for access by Azure tooling.
If you need write access (for creating/modifying issues, applying labels, or merging PRs), you should request to join the 'Azure SDK Partners' group via https://aka.ms/azsdk/join/azuresdkpartners, which requires manager approval. Once approved, you will be added to the 'azure-sdk-partners' GitHub team, granting write access to all Azure SDK-related repos, including azure-rest-api-specs-pr.
For most onboarding and contribution scenarios, joining the Azure organization with public visibility is the minimum requirement.

testcase: How often is the REST API site updated?
query:
title: How often is the REST API site updated?

question: This PR, [backfill /validateResources tsp by majastrz · Pull Request #39669 · Azure/azure-rest-api-specs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/39669), was merged two weeks ago, but the site still hasn't been updated. Please refer to https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/appservice/validate/validate for details.
ground_truth:
The documentation site at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/appservice/validate/validate has not been updated to reflect the merged PR (#39669) because publishing updates to the REST API reference on Microsoft Learn is not instantaneous after a PR merge. The publishing process depends on the correct onboarding and configuration in the azure-rest-api-specs repo, as well as the weekly (typically Tuesday) automated refresh cycle.
Here are the key steps and checks to ensure your changes appear on the documentation site:
Merged to Public Main: Confirm that your PR was merged into the main branch of the public azure-rest-api-specs repository. Only changes merged to public main are picked up for publishing.
README Configuration: Ensure that the readme.md file for your service is present and correctly updated. The README must specify the latest API version and the location of the OpenAPI files. This is required for the automatic refresh process to pick up your changes.
Automatic Refresh Timing: The API reference site is refreshed weekly (on Tuesdays). If your PR was merged less than a week ago, it may not have been picked up yet. If it has been more than a week, check the README configuration and publishing status.
Manual Publishing Request: If you are using an alternative publishing configuration (not the standard README-based approach), or if the automatic refresh did not pick up your changes, you need to submit a publishing request. This can be done by creating a User Story in Azure DevOps or by submitting a PR to the REST API documentation repository to update the publishing configuration.
Onboarding: If this is the first time your API is being published, you must onboard with the Content Publishing Services team. This is a one-time process. Use the onboarding portal at https://learn.microsoft.com/help/onboard/reference?branch=main.
Action Items:
Double-check that your PR was merged to the correct branch and that the README is up to date.
Wait for the next weekly refresh if it has not yet occurred.
If the update still does not appear after the refresh, submit a publishing request or contact the docs team for support

testcase: Azure SDK Release Lifecycle issue
query:
title: Azure SDK Release Lifecycle issue

question: I am planning to create an SDK release plan using the API version 2025-08-18-preview, which includes all the latest type spec changes. Since this API version is a preview, can I still set the Release Lifecycle to GA or Public Preview? If I want to use the GA Release Lifecycle, should I switch to a non-preview API version?
ground_truth:
Yes, you can publish a new public preview (beta) version of an SDK even when a GA version already exists. This is the recommended pattern when you want to add new features:
Keep the existing GA SDK line as-is (no breaking changes).
Release a new beta/public preview SDK version that targets the newer preview (or newer stable) REST API version and includes the new features.
Collect customer feedback on the beta for at least ~1 month.
Once you are confident in the design and quality, graduate that beta line to a new GA SDK version.
Process-wise, you should:
Ensure your new API version is correctly versioned and present in the public specs repo in the right preview/GA folder (per API version lifecycle guidance).
Create a Release Planner release plan for the preview SDK release.
Meet preview SDK test and sample requirements (at least one test and one sample per language for the most common scenario for data plane; at least one sample per language for management plane).
If this is the first-ever release of this package as a preview (for a new namespace/package), you also need an APIView first release approval before releasing the initial beta, but that approval is not required if you’re just doing a new revision of an already-approved GA package. So: you do not need to avoid preview just because a GA exists; instead, you should use a new preview/beta SDK version to introduce those new features safely before rolling them into the next GA.
Our Azure SDK Tools Agent should walk you through it step by step: aka.ms/azsdk/agent

testcase: Support for Internal‑Only Swagger / SDKs for Advanced ARM Resource Properties
query:
title: Support for Internal‑Only Swagger / SDKs for Advanced ARM Resource Properties

question: We’re exposing a new resource in the Network Resource Provider (ARM control-plane) that enables Azure customers to configure their network DDoS policy. We’d like the resource to support a “default” mode for the public offering, and an “Advanced” mode that exposes additional policy knobs intended only for 1P/internal scenarios (not documented publicly, not available to external customers/SDKs by default).
 
Question
Is it possible to maintain an internal-only Swagger/OpenAPI for the same resource that includes the “Advanced” properties, and use that to generate internal-only SDKs for 1P teams?
ground_truth:
Guidance from Azure SDK Architects is that we do not support a single public SDK exposing capabilities that are only callable by 1P while being unavailable to 3P. The SDK mustn't expose an operation that is not callable by 3P. Importantly, “documented” includes anything present in public swagger, public docs, or any official SDK, even if access is later gated by auth or flags. The consistent guidance has been 1P = 3P for public API and SDK surfaces. If functionality is intended for internal-only use, the supported pattern is: 
Keep those properties and operations out of public swagger.
Do not generate or publish official Azure SDKs that surface them.
If needed, use private specs and private SDKs for internal consumption only.
Mixing internal-only features into a public SDK creates broken customer expectations and policy issues, which is why we don’t support that model.

testcase: SDK Validation for API PR's
query:
title: SDK Validation for API PR's

question: I have these lines in my suppressions.yaml 
```
- tool: TypeSpecValidation
  reason: >
    Not ready to generate SDKs from TypeSpec.
    Responsibility: Service team with SDK team collaboration.
    More info: https://aka.ms/azsdk/spec-gen-sdk-config.
  rules: 
  - SdkTspConfigValidation
  paths: 
  - HybridContainerService.Management/tspconfig.yaml
```
But PR is still running SDK validations and failing. Did the suppressions method change?
ground_truth:
The suppression method for TypeSpec SDK validations has not changed, but there are important limitations you need to be aware of. According to the current Azure SDK onboarding guidelines, TypeSpec SDK validation failures cannot be suppressed via PR suppression or permanent suppression (except for TypeSpec Requirement, which can be suppressed in suppressions.yaml). Specifically, 'TypeSpec Validation' checks cannot be suppressed at all. If your PR is failing due to TypeSpec SDK validation, adding entries to suppressions.yaml will not prevent these checks from running or failing your PR.
Your suppressions.yaml entry for 'TypeSpecValidation' will not have any effect on the SDK validation checks. The only suppressible TypeSpec-related check is 'TypeSpec Requirement', and even that must follow the documented suppressions.yaml format. For 'TypeSpec Validation', you must resolve the underlying validation issues directly in your TypeSpec project.
If you are blocked by a validation failure, you should:
Review the error details and address the root cause in your TypeSpec project.
Use the Azure SDK Tools Agent to validate your TypeSpec locally and reproduce the issue.
Only use suppressions.yaml for 'TypeSpec Requirement' (not 'TypeSpec Validation').
Permanent suppression for TypeSpec SDK validation is not supported. Please focus on fixing the validation errors rather than attempting to suppress them.

testcase: Getting an authentication error from apiview.dev when trying to log in
query:
title: Getting an authentication error from apiview.dev when trying to log in

question: After clicking "Login with GitHub" at apiview.dev, I am taken to this GitHub error page.
ground_truth: The ApiView site moved domains: the old apiview.dev is deprecated, so the login error is expected. Use the current site at apiview.org instead and sign in there.

testcase: Question on Required code changes per 'Prepare for Key Vault API version 2026-02-01: Azure RBAC as default access control"
query:
title: Question on Required code changes per 'Prepare for Key Vault API version 2026-02-01: Azure RBAC as default access control"

question: My customer (a large strategic accounts) is preparing the key vault API change. Question on the required code changes:
 
If we instruct all the app teams whose apps interact with Keyvault, how do we know if the SDKs have been updated with the latest Key Vault API version already or not?
 
From https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/developers-guide#apis-and-sdks-for-key-vault-management, there could be many different SDKs, depending what the app is developed on, if I am not mistaken.  What is our messaging to the customer?
ground_truth:
It is our policy that every time a new version of Key Vault service is made publicly available, new stable versions of the Key Vault libraries follow shortly for all languages where the library has been made GA (generally available) 
If you want to check the latest supported versions, the code for each language contains an enum where this is specified. 
confirmed that the latest version of the Key Vault service supported by most of our libraries is 7.6, with V2025_07_01 being the sole outlier in .NET at this time. That said, most developers would do well in always using the latest versions of both the service and the libraries, as they almost exclusively receive additive updates. Breaking changes are an extremely rare occurrence, I think I've only seen this happen once for the way we handled KV authentication.
 
If you ever want to check out which specific service version is supported by a given library version, you can check out the classes such as the following:
Go: Client
Java: SecretServiceVersion
JavaScript/TypeScript: ApiVersion
.NET: SecretClientOptions.ServiceVersion
Python: SecretClientOptions
These examples are for Key Vault Secrets, but this applies for their equivalents in Key Vault Certificates, and Key Vault Keys as well.
 
If you want to check out versions supported by a specific release, you can search for the corresponding GitHub tag in our GitHub releases, think of it as snapshot of our repo at that time. For example, in Java the latest version for Secrets is 4.10.5, this is the corresponding release and tag. This is what you would see when searching for this version on the Java releases page

testcase: Enum value change with new API version
query:
title: Enum value change with new API version

question: We have released an SDK with API version 2025-07-01-preview with an API enum that had values X and Y. We plan to scratch Y and and add a new value Z under a new preview version of SDK 2026-03-01-preview with enum values being Y and Z. Is this acceptable with preview versions?
ground_truth:
Yes, it is generally acceptable to remove an enum value (Y) and add a new value (Z) in a new preview API version (2026-03-01-preview), as long as the change is made in a new API version and not within the same version. Azure guidelines and TypeSpec versioning allow breaking changes between preview versions, especially when introducing a new preview version. However, you must ensure that:
The breaking change (removal of Y) is documented and communicated, as it may impact customers using the previous preview version.
The new preview version (2026-03-01-preview) has a later date than the previous preview (2025-07-01-preview).
Only the latest preview version is active in TypeSpec, and older previews are retired as per Azure policy.
Breaking changes between preview versions are allowed, but breaking changes relative to a stable (GA) version are discouraged and require review.
If you are using TypeSpec, you should mark the new preview version with @previewVersion and update the enum accordingly. If you are maintaining OpenAPI specs, ensure the new version reflects the updated enum values and the old preview version is retired from the specs repo if no longer needed for validation or registration.
In summary, breaking changes like removing enum values are allowed between preview versions, but not within the same version, and must be accompanied by a new api-version. Always follow Azure's retirement and communication policies for preview APIs.

testcase: Control plane SDK ownership
query:
title: Control plane SDK ownership

question: Can someone from the Azure SDK team chime in on clarifying ownership for control plane SDKs?  My understanding was that Dataplane SDK was owned by the service teams but control plane SDK was owned by Azure SDK team themselves?  
Is that understanding correct? If so, can you please share details on how to release a new version of control plane SDK?
ground_truth:
Ownership
Data plane (client plane) SDKs: Owned by the service team from spec through design, implementation, tests, and release. They must go through Architecture Board review before first beta.
Management / control plane (ARM) SDKs: The Azure SDK team owns the common tooling, architecture guidelines, and language repos, but the service team owns the actual release for their resource provider. For TypeSpec-based management-plane SDKs, service teams also own triggering generation and merging SDK PRs via Release Planner.
Releasing a new version of a control‑plane (management‑plane) SDK
Prerequisites
REST API spec changes merged into Azure/azure-rest-api-specs main.
TypeSpec is used for new services; OpenAPI is still supported for existing ones.
Required access to GitHub and Azure DevOps, and a release plan created.
All SDK release prerequisites met (tests, samples, etc.).
Create and use a release plan (mandatory)
Create a management-plane release plan in Release Planner.
Complete the API readiness milestone.
Complete the SDK release milestone (this is also required for CPEX PP/GA sign‑off).
Generate or update the SDK
TypeSpec management plane: • Use the Generate SDK step in the release plan. This creates draft PRs in each language repo. • Service team validates content, pushes any fixes, and when ready selects Ready for review to request Azure SDK team review. • Azure SDK team reviews; service team addresses feedback and merges the SDK PRs.
OpenAPI management plane: • SDK generation happens when you request a release as part of the management-plane release plan for Java, JS, Python, Go; .NET follows its own mgmt generation doc.
Request and schedule the release
Management plane release timing: • TypeSpec: on‑demand. • OpenAPI: request before the monthly cutoff (2nd Friday); release is typically 4th Friday.
Use the release plan to request the release for your target languages.
Run the release pipelines
Once Release Planner shows ready to release, service team: • Ensures the service version is deployed. • Starts the SDK release pipeline in Azure DevOps for each language (or follows the .NET-specific mgmt release process).
After publishing, review and merge the version‑bump and release‑notes PRs that the pipelines open in the language repos.
Mark completion
Verify packages on the public feeds (metadata, downloads, documentation).
Mark the Release SDK task complete in the release plan to finish the SDK release milestone.
If you need more detailed help for your specific RP and languages, you can:
Use the Azure SDK Tools Agent (azsdk-tools-mcp) from VS Code to walk through release plan and generation steps.
Contact the Azure SDK Release Support or language-specific Teams channels listed in the docs.

testcase: How do I get permission to run `/azp run` in powershell and CLI repos?
query:
title: How do I get permission to run `/azp run` in powershell and CLI repos?

question: Hi folks,
 
I am in the SDK Partners write access github team, but I do not have access to run pipelines with `/azp run` in the CLI and Powershell. What do I need to do to be able to run them?
 
Another question: in the `azure-rest-api-specs` repo, when I run `/azp run` it will show me a message that I must specify a particular pipeline. However, I do not know how to specify the name or even get the list of pipeline names. In that repo `/azp run` does not run all the pipelines - in fact it will run none for me.
ground_truth:
For the `azure-rest-api-specs` repo, **pushing another commit is the proper solution** — many checks now use GitHub Actions instead of DevOps, so `/azp run` no longer applies. Push an empty commit or merge from the parent branch to rerun checks.

For CLI and PowerShell repos, those are **not managed by the Azure SDK team**. You need to contact the CLI team directly. Their Teams team is: [Azure CLIs partners](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3ADNHD30SQx04UX9PnCIQ5d4fum1t28Mvjx5RJkrSBNBU1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=9a661795-1b01-4a5b-9dd5-be571422334c&tenantId=72f988bf-86f1-41af-91ab-2d7cd011db47), specifically the [Azure CLI PR Review](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/channel/19%3A8432b6805ffc476b9130f5355a67991b%40thread.tacv2/Azure%20CLI%20PR%20Review?groupId=9a661795-1b01-4a5b-9dd5-be571422334c&tenantId=72f988bf-86f1-41af-91ab-2d7cd011db47) channel for PR-related questions.

---

testcase: Azure Image Builder API Release
query:
title: Azure Image Builder API Release

question: Hello,
 
I'm the PM for Azure Image Builder and we have a new API version we're trying to release within the next few weeks to ship some critical customer features. I started a release using release planner and have a couple of questions:

- How quickly could we get our ARM API review completed, assuming we were to define our service APIs and get our PRs in next week?
- Is there any way to streamline this process to get the release done sooner?
ground_truth:
For ARM (management-plane) APIs, there is no published guaranteed turnaround time; reviews are driven through the PR workflow in the specs repo. To streamline: finalize and validate the spec now (including running local style/breaking-change checks), get the PR into the specs repo with CI clean and no blocking labels, and keep the release plan and API readiness milestone fully updated. Use ARM Office Hours only if blocked after those steps.

Importantly, Azure now requires TypeSpec Azure for all API definitions — direct OpenAPI/Swagger authoring is legacy and exception-only. For a new ARM API version, use TypeSpec Azure as the source of truth, generate OpenAPI, and commit both. PRs for new API versions not based on TypeSpec will be blocked starting Q4 FY26, and the brownfield transition deadline is March 30, 2026. Create the spec PR against `main` using the **Control Plane (ARM) API** PR template.

---

testcase: Hi General, We have a TypeSpec model where a resource `Job` has a `properties`...
query:
title: Hi General, We have a TypeSpec model where a resource `Job` has a `properties`...

question: Hi General,
 
We have a TypeSpec model where a resource `Job` has a `properties` field that is a discriminated union (JobProeprties, with subtypes CommandJob, `SweepJob`, etc.):
 
```
model Job {
  name: string;
  properties: JobProperties;  // discriminated union
}

@discriminator("jobType")
model JobProperties { ... }

model CommandJob extends JobProperties { ... }
model SweepJob extends JobProperties { ... }
```

 
Currently customers must unwrap the envelope and cast:
 
```
result = client.jobs.get(name="my-job")
props = cast(CommandJob, result.properties)
```

We want customers to work with a flat object directly:
 
```
result = client.jobs.get(name="my-job")
result.command   # no unwrapping, no cast
```

We explored [flattenProperty](https://azure.github.io/typespec-azure/docs/libraries/typespec-client-generator-core/reference/decorators/#@Azure.ClientGenerator.Core.Legacy.flattenProperty) but it is explicitly blocked on discriminated unions.
 
Question: Is there any TypeSpec decorator or emitter customization that achieves flattening of a discriminated union property without writing per-language custom patch code?
ground_truth:
There is no supported way today to have the TypeSpec-based emitters automatically flatten a discriminated union property into distinct top-level, subtype-specific members on a containing model. `@flattenProperty` is explicitly legacy and not supported for discriminated unions. The recommended approach is to keep the wire shape as `Job { properties: JobProperties }` with `@discriminator("jobType")` on the base, let clients expose `result.properties` as a polymorphic type, and rely on idiomatic narrowing/casting in each language. Any projection like `result.command` would require emitter-specific customization per language, which is outside the standard TypeSpec/Azure emitters. For further discussion, post in the TypeSpec Discussion channel where the relevant experts have visibility.

---

testcase: Permission to push branch denied
query:
title: Permission to push branch denied

question: Hey,
I'm trying to push a new branch for a PR, but get "Permission to Azure/azure-rest-api-specs.git denied to OmriBurdman." with 403.
I joined the relevant orgs in GitHub, but still waiting for approval on request to join "Azure SDK Partners" in CoreIdentity. Is that the reason I lack permissions? Who can approve the request?
thanks
ground_truth:
The likely issue is that the required GitHub membership must be public rather than private. Access requests to join `azure sdk partners` are approved by the user's manager, but in this case the user also already appears to be in the `azure-sdk-write` GitHub team. The next things to verify are that the membership is public and that the access propagation has completed.

---

testcase: Deletion of Old APIs
query:
title: Deletion of Old APIs

question: QQ: We deprecated some APIs from 2022 + there's an api that we merged into azure-rest-specs main, that we never really "released" officially. i.e we have no customers using it + no cli/sdk support. Is it ok to delete them from the repo? Or is there a different guidance on what to do with these APIs
ground_truth:
You can delete deprecated APIs, but treat this as a proper retirement following the standard guidance. Open a draft PR deleting the APIs to see what the PR checks report. Keep API deprecation, folder migration to v2, and new API version additions in **separate PRs** rather than combining them.

For specs that are currently v1/Swagger in public main and already converted to TypeSpec in RPSaaSMaster: do not attempt to convert to TypeSpec or v2 folder structure yourself. Instead, keep using v1/Swagger for now and wait for the central Azure SDK team to perform the conversion. Focus on syncing main with RPSaaSMaster by copying over the API versions you've officially released, using smaller, incremental PRs (e.g., one PR for deprecation/removal, another for adding new API versions).

---

testcase: Missing webapplicationfirewall in 2025-05-01
query:
title: Missing webapplicationfirewall in 2025-05-01

question: Prajjwal Kamboj / Others : I am seeing webapplicationfirewall.json present in 2025-03-01 but missing in 2025-05-01? Is this some regression
03-01 API version : [https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/d12a591062fc42adbad8c0105094f3de018a71b7/specifi…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/d12a591062fc42adbad8c0105094f3de018a71b7/specification/network/resource-manager/Microsoft.Network/Network/stable/2025-03-01/webapplicationfirewall.json)
05-01 missing folder : [https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/tree/d12a591062fc42adbad8c0105094f3de018a71b7/specifi…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/tree/d12a591062fc42adbad8c0105094f3de018a71b7/specification/network/resource-manager/Microsoft.Network/Network/stable/2025-05-01)
ground_truth:
This is expected behavior from TypeSpec conversion, not a regression. All APIs from `webapplicationfirewall.json` have been merged into `applicationGateway.json` in the 2025-05-01 version. API-level functional equivalence is guaranteed during TypeSpec conversion, but retaining specific Swagger file names/structure is not part of the process.

However, the merge caused a temporary issue with public REST API documentation on learn.microsoft.com — the WAF docs were not loading. This was identified as a bug in the docs generation pipeline, and the docs team (Yanan Zhao) is working on a fix. The expected resolution is within a week.

---

testcase: Determining the API Version to use in Powershell
query:
title: Determining the API Version to use in Powershell

question: Hello! I am from Microsoft.Sql, and I would like a bit of assistance in determining how the powershell SDK picks an API version. I was under the impression that this was based on the readme in our azure-rest-api-specs setup, but I have some doubts now. Could someone point me to how the version is selected? Specifically, I am looking at the `Get-AzureSqlServerFirewallRule` commandlet — it seems to be using the 2014 version, but I am not sure why.
ground_truth:
PowerShell is not a product owned by the Azure SDK team. The API version used by a PowerShell cmdlet is hard-coded in the .NET management client assembly it was built against, not dynamically chosen from the specs README at runtime. For questions about PowerShell API version selection, contact the PowerShell CLC KPI owners (wangalex and jingweiwang) who can point you in the right direction.

---

testcase: Not Allowed to Create PR
query:
title: Not Allowed to Create PR

question: Hello, 
I have followed the enghub doc on getting access to the repo: [Get access to Azure SDKs | Azure SDK](https://eng.ms/docs/products/azure-developer-experience/onboard/access)
 
But when joining the Azure SDK Partners group through coreIdentity, I could only join by selecting 'BuildAndRelease', so I could not perform these steps listed here: 
 
It has been over a day since requesting access to Azure SDK Partners, and I see it in my enlistments:
 
But I am still not allowed to create a PR or fork the repo: 
 
when hovering over new pull request button i see a forbidden sign:
 
I am part of the Microsoft and Azure organizations on GitHub but was not able to see the described UI that mentions if my membership is public or private when following the steps, but i do think it is public. 
 
Am I missing anything?
ground_truth:
You need to use a regular GitHub.com account, not a GitHub Enterprise (EMU) account. GitHub auto-login may have signed you into the enterprise account, which cannot fork or submit PRs to public Azure repos. Sign out and sign in with your personal/OSS GitHub.com account. You don't actually need special account permissions or org membership to fork and submit a PR to a public repo — once you're using the correct account type, the standard fork + PR workflow will work.

---

testcase: Question regarding creating a stable version
query:
title: Question regarding creating a stable version

question: Hi team,
 
Our team is looking to start a stable version based off current preview version 2026-02-01-preview. We have some of the features tested in 2026-02-01-preview but some are not yet fully tested. We would like to know what we should do in our case:
 

- Start a new stable version 2026-06-01 with all tested apis/features. Keep 2026-02-01-preivew and continue to test and iterate on top. Later we can create a new stable version (2026-09-01) for example for the remaining features.
- Start a new stable version 2026-06-01 with all tested apis/features. Create a new preview version from 2026-02-01-preview and continue iteration. Later we can create a new stable version (2026-09-01) for example for the remaining features.
- We need to bring all apis/features from 2026-02-01-preview to stable version 2026-06-01. Keep 2026-02-01-preivew for future iteration. 
- We need to bring all apis/features from 2026-02-01-preview to stable version 2026-06-01. We also need to have a new preview version for future iteration.
ground_truth:
Go with option (2): create a new stable version 2026-06-01 with only the preview changes that are fully tested and ready for GA, and at the same time branch a new preview version for the remaining work. The new preview version should be based on 2026-06-01 plus the preview features that didn't ship — not stacked on top of 2026-02-01-preview.

With small exceptions, all API versions are considered "frozen" after merging to `main` or `RPSaaSMaster`, even previews — so any substantive change (including adding new endpoints) requires a new preview API version. Note that API versions behave like semver: you cannot define a `2026-06-01-preview` as a prelude to `2026-06-01`; the preview and stable versions must be completely separate (e.g. `2026-09-01-preview`, or even `2026-06-02-preview`). Dates are largely arbitrary targets. See the breaking-changes policy at [aka.ms/AzBreakingChangesPolicy](https://aka.ms/AzBreakingChangesPolicy).

---

testcase: Release Planner app is being sunsetted (phased): what you need to know
query:
title: Release Planner app is being sunsetted (phased): what you need to know

question: **TL;DR:** The Release Planner **PowerApp** is being retired in phases. Starting **June 6, 2026**, release plan creation/management in the app will be replaced by banners directing you to the Azure SDK Tools Agent. Onboarding will continue to work in the app for a few more weeks before transitioning to a new GitHub Issue workflow. Full decommission by **July 7, 2026**.

What is the replacement, what is the phased schedule, and what should service teams do now?
ground_truth:
Release plans themselves are not going away — only the surface where you create and manage them. The replacement is the **Azure SDK Tools Agent** ([aka.ms/azsdk/agent](https://aka.ms/azsdk/agent)) combined with the **Release Plan Dashboard** ([aka.ms/azsdk/releaseplan-dashboard](https://aka.ms/azsdk/releaseplan-dashboard)). The agent designs APIs with TypeSpec, generates SDKs for the top languages (.NET, Java, Python, JavaScript, Go), and manages release plans/attestation/CPEX KPIs end-to-end from VS Code (GitHub Copilot) or the CLI.

Phased schedule:
- **Phase 1 (June 6, 2026):** PowerApp release-plan creation/management disabled; banners redirect to the Agent.
- **Phase 2 (~2 weeks after June 6):** PowerApp onboarding disabled, replaced by a new GitHub Issue template/workflow.
- **Phase 3 (July 7, 2026):** PowerApp fully removed.

What to do now: if you have an active release in progress, your existing release plan is on the Release Plan Dashboard — no action needed. For new release plans, use the Azure SDK Tools Agent. For new-service onboarding, continue using the existing Release Planner flow until Phase 2. If the Agent asks for a Product Tree ID and you don't have one, you can point the agent at the API spec path for now. Post questions/feedback in the [AzSDK Tools Agent](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/channel/19%3A6d2c19322c254a80bcc521675134da03%40thread.skype/AzSDK%20Tools%20Agent?groupId=3e17dcb0-4257-4a30-b843-77f47f1d4121&tenantId=72f988bf-86f1-41af-91ab-2d7cd011db47) channel.

testcase: Question about maintaining private repo main branch
query:
title: Question about maintaining private repo main branch

question: Hi team,
 
I have a generic question regarding maintaining [**azure-rest-api-specs-pr**](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr) main branch.
From this documentation [REST API specifications (either OpenAPIv2 or TypeSpec) | ARM Wiki](https://eng.ms/docs/products/arm/rpaas/swaggeronboarding), I see for RPaaS onboarded RPs, the branch that mentioned are RPSaaSDev for private RP, RPSaaSMaster for dogfood/Canary/AFEC, and public Main for Canary and Production all subscriptions. Does this mean we don't need to maintain and check in our PRs to azure-rest-api-specs-pr's main branch at all? Or for anything that we want to publish to public Main, we should still check into azure-rest-api-specs-pr's main to keep parity?
ground_truth:
The `main` branch of the private `azure-rest-api-specs-pr` repo is mirrored from the `main` branch of the public `azure-rest-api-specs` repo daily — no commits can be merged directly to `specs-pr/main`. So you do not need to maintain or target `azure-rest-api-specs-pr/main` yourself. For RPaaS, use `RPSaaSDev` for private development and `RPSaaSMaster` for dogfood/Canary/AFEC; public releases still go through `azure-rest-api-specs/main`. However, each spec owner is responsible for porting any changes between `public/main` and `private/RPSaaSMaster`.
